text: In Git, branching is a powerful way to manage your code changes without affecting the main project. Typically, developers create feature branches to work on new features or bug fixes separately. Once the work is complete, the changes are merged back into the main branch, often called "main" or "master." This process follows a version control workflow that helps teams collaborate efficiently. Before merging, teams commonly use pull requests to review the code, discuss improvements, and ensure quality. This systematic approach keeps the main branch stable while allowing experimentation and innovation in separate branches.
